original_abstract:
During the fall of 2010, concurrent measurements of particle hygroscopic growth at 90% relative humidity, cloud condensation nuclei (CCN) activity, and size-resolved chemical composition were conducted in the Thüringer Wald mountain range of central Germany. Throughout the sampling period, the median hygroscopicity parameters ($\kappa$) derived from hygroscopicity measurements were 0.14, 0.14, 0.17, 0.21, 0.24, and 0.28 for particles with diameters of 50, 75, 100, 150, 200, and 250 nm, respectively. Closure between HTDMA-measured ($\kappa_{HTDMA}$) and chemical composition-derived ($\kappa_{chem}$) hygroscopicity was investigated using the Zdanovskii–Stokes–Robinson (ZSR) mixing rule. We found that using size-averaged chemical composition led to a substantial overprediction of $\kappa$ values (by 30% to 40% for 100 and 150 nm particles); however, incorporating size-resolved composition significantly improved the closure. Discrepancies between predicted and measured growth were partly attributed to the evaporation of NH$_4$NO$_3$ within the HTDMA system. Additionally, the hygroscopicity of the organic fraction ($\kappa_{org}$) showed a positive correlation with the O:C ratio ($\kappa_{org}$ = 0.19 × (O:C) − 0.03), providing a useful framework for defining organic hygroscopicity in closure studies.

While $\kappa$ values derived from CCN measurements were approximately 30% higher than those determined from hygroscopic growth (considering the hydrophilic mode), this difference likely reflects the influence of surface tension, solution non-ideality, gas-particle partitioning of semivolatile species, and the effects of partially soluble or undissolved matter. Consequently, extrapolating particle properties from sub-saturated HTDMA data to the point of activation requires caution. Finally, a closure study between CCN-measured ($\kappa_{CCN}$) and chemically derived ($\kappa_{CCN, chem}$) values demonstrated that $\kappa_{CCN}$ can be accurately predicted using size-resolved chemical information and the ZSR mixing rule.
humanized_abstract:
Hygroscopic growth of aerosol particles in a German mountain forest during fall We measured the hygroscopic growth of aerosol particles at 90% relative humidity, their cloud condensation nuclei (CCN) activity and their size resolved chemical composition in the Thüringer Wald mountain forest in central Germany during fall 2010. The median values of the hygroscopic growth factor (GF) at dry conditions ( κ) derived from hygroscopicity measurements of particles with dry diameters of 50, 75, 100, 150, 200 and 250 nm were 0.14, 0.14, 0.17, 0.21, 0.24 and 0.28, respectively. We investigated the closure between hygroscopic growth factors derived from the horizontal tandem differential mobility analyser (HTDMA, κHTMDA) and those derived from the chemical composition of particles (κchem) using the Zdanovskii–Stokes–Robinson (ZSR) model. While the size averaged chemical composition led to an overprediction of κHTMDA by 30% to 40% for particles with dry diameters of 100 and 150 nm, good agreement between κchem and κHTMDA was achieved by using the size resolved chemical composition. Possible reasons for the observed discrepancies are evaporation of NH4NO3 in the HTDMA, which could lead to lower growth factors, and the assumption of the ZSR model concerning the organics, i.e. their volume fraction and the relationship between their volume fraction and κ. For the latter we derived a relationship between the hygroscopicity of the organics (κorg) and the oxygen to carbon ratio (O:C) of the organic mass, given by κorg = 0.19 × (O:C) − 0.03.

The κ derived from CCN measurements was found to be approximately 30% higher than those derived from hygroscopic growth (excluding the hydrophilic mode). Reasons for this discrepancy include effects due to high surface tension, solution non-ideality, semivolatile gases partitioned onto particles and effects due to partially soluble or insoluble components. Great care is then needed when extrapolating data derived under sub-saturated HTDMA conditions to the saturation level required for CCN activation. A closure study is performed between the κ derived from CCN analysis (κCCN) and the κ derived from laboratory measurements (κCCN, chem) where the latter was shown to be highly predictable based on a combination of size-segregated analysis of the components that comprise CCN activity and the ZSR mixing rule.

document.output:

document.input:
During the fall of 2010, concurrent measurements of particle hygroscopic growth at 90% relative humidity, cloud condensation nuclei (CCN) activity, and size-resolved chemical composition were conducted in the Thüringer Wald mountain range of central Germany. Throughout the sampling period, the median hygroscopicity parameters ($\kappa$) derived from hygroscopicity measurements were 0.14, 0.14, 0.17, 0.21, 0.24, and 0.28 for particles with diameters of 50, 75, 100, 150, 200, and 250 nm, respectively. Finally, a closure study between CCN-measured ($\kappa_{CCN}$) and chemically derived ($\kappa_{CCN, chem}$) values demonstrated that $\kappa_{CCN}$ can be accurately predicted using size-resolved chemical information and the ZSR mixing rule.
